title: Te Hauora o te Kōawa: The Living Health of Our Stream / Te Hauora o te Kōawa: Te Ora Ohorere o Tō Tātou Awa
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the story of Te Kōawa, the stream that flows through our rohe and nourishes the whenua of Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will explore how the mauri (life force) of our awa connects to their own hauora (wellbeing) through traditional practices of water guardianship, navigation, and environmental balance that have sustained our people for generations.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain the four dimensions of hauora (te whare tapa whā) through the lens of Te Kōawa's ecosystem
Measure and analyze water quality indicators using both mātauranga Māori and scientific methods
Create a personal hauora action plan that incorporates traditional practices of environmental care
Demonstrate respectful engagement with local kaumātua and environmental experts through prepared questions and active listening

activities.title: Whakatau: Karakia ki te Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia led by a local kaumātua at Te Kōawa stream, acknowledging the mauri of the water and our connection to it. Students will learn proper protocols for approaching and respecting waterways.

activities.title: He Whare Tapa Whā o Te Kōawa
activities.description: Students map the health of Te Kōawa using the four dimensions of hauora: taha wairua (spiritual - mauri of the water), taha hinengaro (mental - water clarity), taha tinana (physical - water temperature/quality), taha whānau (social - community use of the stream).

activities.title: Ngā Tohu Hauora Wai
activities.description: Practical water testing activity where students use both traditional indicators (presence of native species, water clarity) and scientific tools (pH strips, thermometers) to assess the health of Te Kōawa.

activities.title: Kōrero mai, Kōrero atu
activities.description: Students interview local kaumātua and environmental kaitiaki about traditional practices for maintaining water health and how these practices connect to human wellbeing.

activities.title: Te Mahere Hauora
activities.description: Students create personal hauora action plans that incorporate at least two traditional practices learned (e.g., regular visits to the awa, participating in stream clean-ups, learning water-related karakia).

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith (local water knowledge holder)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Wai Ora' water testing kits
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Māori concepts of health'
Local stream health monitoring data from Mangakōtukutuku Environmental Trust

tasks: Completed Te Kōawa hauora mapping worksheet
Water quality testing data analysis report

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 - Personal health and physical development
Health and Physical Education: D1 - Healthy communities and environments
Science: Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat
Social Studies: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
